text.title: Internationalization (i18n) Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules grounding the internationalization methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with sourced rationale; the final rule is the skip-this-methodology gate referenced by the decision tree.

rule.statement: All translatable strings use ICU MessageFormat keys via t('key', {vars}). Concatenated strings are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Plural and gender rules differ per locale; ICU is the only correct way to express them.

rule.statement: Dates / numbers / currency formatted via Intl.DateTimeFormat / NumberFormat / Babel format_currency. No manual string concatenation.
rule.rationale: Manual formatting breaks the moment locale changes (decimal separator, currency placement).

rule.statement: CSS uses logical properties (margin-inline-start, padding-block-end). Physical properties (margin-left, padding-top) are linted-forbidden on layout components.
rule.rationale: Logical properties flip automatically in RTL contexts; physical ones break Arabic and Hebrew layouts.

rule.statement: CI runs a key-drift check: every key in the base locale (en.json) must exist in every other locale. Missing keys fail CI.
rule.rationale: Drift produces silent fallbacks to en, leading to mixed-language UIs.

rule.statement: PRs introducing hardcoded user-facing English strings are rejected; all must go through t().
rule.rationale: Once a hardcoded string ships, it is invisible to translators; rejection is cheaper than archeology.

rule.statement: Fallback locale chain is explicit: tag→language root→en. Document the chain.
rule.rationale: Implicit fallback chains produce surprising mixed-language UIs in edge cases.

rule.statement: Single-locale forever, static brochure, or <20 chrome strings — skip this methodology.
rule.rationale: i18n overhead requires ongoing translation pipeline that small surfaces cannot justify.
